summary:
The First Anglo-Afghan War (1839–1842) was a catastrophic British military venture driven by fears of Russian expansion toward India. Britain invaded Afghanistan to replace the reigning Dost Mohammad Khan with a pro-British ruler, succeeded initially, then faced a nationwide uprising that destroyed almost the entire British-Indian occupying force in one of the worst disasters in British military history.

The war was part of the [[event:2627|Great Game]] between Britain and Russia for dominance in Central Asia. British India feared that Russian influence over Afghanistan could threaten the 'jewel in the crown.' In 1839, a British-Indian force of about 21,000 men invaded, occupied Kabul, and installed Shah Shuja on the Afghan throne. For two years the occupation appeared stable, but the Afghans deeply resented the presence of foreign infidels and the displacement of their legitimate ruler.

In November 1841 a popular uprising in Kabul killed the British political agent Alexander Burnes. The British commander, Elphinstone, was indecisive and ultimately negotiated a withdrawal. In January 1842 the entire British-Indian garrison of approximately 16,500 people — soldiers, camp followers, and dependents — left Kabul in mid-winter under assurances of safe passage. They were attacked repeatedly in the mountain passes by Afghan tribesmen. Only one man, Dr. William Brydon, reached Jalalabad on horseback; a handful of others were taken prisoner. The near-total annihilation of the Kabul force shocked British India and the world.

Britain eventually sent a punitive expedition that retook Kabul and symbolically demolished the Kabul bazaar before withdrawing. Dost Mohammad was restored. The war established a pattern that would be repeated in subsequent Afghan conflicts: foreign forces could occupy Afghanistan but could not pacify it against determined Afghan resistance.
